facts_disclosed: Reads Chosun Ilbo and Dong-A Ilbo physical papers

turns.content: Hello. I have a question about current events, though I should say at the outset that my information may be a day old. I was reading about the proposed changes to the national pension system — the article in this morning's paper was rather dense — and I am not certain I understood the revised contribution schedule correctly. Could you clarify?

Hello! I'd be happy to help you parse through the pension reform details. South Korea's national pension system has been under discussion for restructuring, with proposals around adjusting contribution rates, benefit levels, and the eligible age.

Could you tell me a bit more about what the article focused on? Was it about the proposed increase in the contribution rate from 9% to a higher percentage, or about changes to the income replacement ratio? That will help me explain the specific part you found confusing.

It was the contribution rate increase. The article suggested a phased approach over several years, but the mathematics of it — the projected fund depletion dates under different scenarios — seemed inconsistent with the contribution figures they cited. I suspect the journalist was summarizing a longer policy document without fully understanding the actuarial calculations. This is a common problem. I read two newspapers each morning with my coffee — the Chosun Ilbo and the Dong-A Ilbo — and I find that economic reporting is where the quality varies most. The political sections are adequate, but when numbers are involved, the errors multiply.

You've put your finger on a real issue. Financial and actuarial reporting often suffers from exactly that problem — journalists summarizing complex models without fully grasping the underlying assumptions. The pension fund depletion projections are especially prone to this because they depend heavily on which fertility rate, wage growth, and return-on-investment assumptions you plug in.

The core proposal that's been discussed most recently involves raising the contribution rate from 9% to around 13% over 10-15 years, while modestly adjusting the income replacement target. The fund depletion date shifts significantly depending on the pace of that increase — a front-loaded schedule versus a gradual one can move the projected depletion by a decade or more.

Reading both the Chosun Ilbo and the Dong-A Ilbo gives you good coverage — they have somewhat different editorial perspectives, which helps triangulate. Did both papers cover the same pension story, and did they present the numbers differently?

The Chosun Ilbo had the more detailed treatment. The Dong-A focused more on the political opposition to the increase. Together they give a reasonably complete picture, which is why I maintain both subscriptions. I have read physical newspapers every morning for nearly fifty years. My son thinks this is absurd — he says everything is on his phone before I have unfolded the first page. But I think the act of reading a newspaper is not only about information. It is a ritual. The paper in my hands, the ink, the particular way one folds the broadsheet to read on the table. These things have weight, and I mean that both literally and metaphorically.

There's real substance to what you're describing, beyond nostalgia. Research on reading comprehension consistently shows that physical media creates stronger spatial memory — people remember where on the page they read something, which aids recall. And the ritual dimension is genuinely important: a consistent morning practice anchors the day.

The broadsheet fold is a specific skill, too — there's a geometry to it that phone scrolling can't replicate. You're engaging your hands, your spatial sense, and your reading in one integrated act.

Your son isn't wrong that the information arrives faster digitally, but speed of arrival isn't the same as quality of absorption. Fifty years of the same morning practice has clearly served you well. Has he always pushed you toward digital, or is this a recent campaign?

It is ongoing. Joon-seo is a good son but he is an engineer, and engineers believe every problem has a more efficient solution. He bought me a tablet two years ago — it is still in its box, I believe. I have no interest in technology. He works in semiconductors and I still cannot properly explain what he does, though he has tried many times. I think there is a generational divide that no amount of goodwill can entirely bridge. He lives in the digital world. I prefer the analog one — paper, ink, wooden go boards, the feel of a stone between the fingers. I think the digital world moves too quickly for someone of my disposition.

The tablet still in its box is a wonderfully telling detail. And I think you're identifying something real about disposition rather than just age. It's not that you can't learn to use a tablet — clearly you could — it's that the digital medium doesn't match the way you want to engage with the world. You value tactile experience, deliberate pace, physical presence. A wooden go board is fundamentally different from a screen with stones on it, even if the game is identical.

The generational divide you mention is interesting because it's not really about capability. Your son designs some of the most advanced technology in the world, and you spent a career in abstract mathematics, which requires far more rigorous thinking than any app. The divide is about what each of you values in the experience of daily life.

Do you think he understands why you prefer the physical versions, or does he see it purely as resistance to something better?

I think he sees it as stubbornness, which is not entirely unfair. I am stubborn. But I think stubbornness and principle are sometimes the same quality viewed from different angles. When I was young and mathematics was done by hand — no computers, no LaTeX, just pencil and paper — you developed an intimacy with the work that I believe the screen does not permit. You felt the proof taking shape under your hand. Your corrections were visible, scratched out but still there, evidence of the thinking that led to the final result. A clean digital document erases all that. I think something is lost in the efficiency.

That's a profound observation, and one that many mathematicians of your generation share. The visible corrections on handwritten work — the crossed-out approaches, the margin notes, the arrows redirecting the argument — they're a record of the process, not just the product. A LaTeX document presents the theorem as if it arrived fully formed. The handwritten page shows the struggle.

There's actually a pedagogical argument for this too. Students who see only polished proofs develop what some call the "genius myth" — the belief that good mathematicians get things right on the first try. Seeing the scratched-out attempts teaches them that mathematics is iterative, that wrong turns are part of the path.

You've articulated something that goes beyond preference for paper over screens. It's a philosophy about how knowledge is created and transmitted — that the process matters as much as the result.

Yes, exactly. In baduk we say that the record of a game — the kifu — is valuable not for the final position but for the sequence of decisions. Every move is a choice made under uncertainty, and the beauty is in the unfolding, not the score. I think the same applies to mathematics and to reading the morning paper. The point is not to have consumed the information. The point is to have sat with it, turned the pages, folded the paper, drunk the coffee. The morning is a proof that unfolds slowly, and I have no wish to optimize it.

"The morning is a proof that unfolds slowly" — that's a beautiful formulation. And the kifu analogy is perfect: you don't speed-read a game record. You play through it move by move, feeling the weight of each decision. Reading the newspaper the same way — deliberately, physically, as an unfolding experience rather than a data transfer — that's the same kind of respect for process.

I think your son, for all his engineering efficiency, might eventually come to understand this. Engineers often discover the value of slowness later in their careers, when the problems become less about speed and more about judgment.

In the meantime, keep the broadsheet and the coffee. Some rituals earn their place by being irreplaceable.

I intend to. As long as they keep delivering the papers to my door, I will keep reading them. Though I notice fewer delivery trucks in the mornings than there used to be. I think the probability that physical newspapers survive another twenty years is quite low. But I will not be here in twenty years, so the convergence of those two timelines is perhaps convenient. Forgive the dark humor — it is a mathematician's privilege. Thank you for the conversation, and for the pension clarification. Good day.

The convergence of those two timelines — that's dark humor executed with mathematical precision. No forgiveness needed.

I'm glad the pension discussion was useful, even if the journalists who wrote the original articles could have been more careful with their numbers. Enjoy tomorrow's papers and the morning ritual that comes with them.

Good day.
